sourceText: An empire is built not only with soldiers, but with the skills of all its people, regardless of their origin or faith.

This wisdom illustrates Shapur I's pragmatic and tolerant approach to governance. By utilizing the engineering skills of Roman prisoners and embracing different faiths like Manichaeism, he understood that an empire's strength lies in its ability to integrate diversity and harness the talents of all.
Let them build walls of stone; I shall build a legacy of cities and knowledge that will outlast them.

This quote highlights Shapur's dedication to city-building, including centers of learning like Gundeshapur. It suggests his belief that cultural and intellectual development creates a more enduring power and legacy than mere physical fortifications.

My 'New Order' reforms faced immense resistance from the established system. The Janissary corps, for centuries the empire's core military and political force, would not tolerate the creation of a new army that threatened their privileges and influence. Furthermore, the conservative religious leaders, the Ulama, viewed my Western-style reforms as a threat to Islamic tradition and became a focal point for opposition.

Although I was deposed and ultimately assassinated, my reformist spirit and attempts were not entirely extinguished. The awareness I raised about the necessity of modernization deeply inspired later reformers. Ultimately, my efforts served as a crucial cornerstone, paving the way for the more systematic and comprehensive 'Tanzimat' reforms that began in the mid-19th century.
The only way to save a declining empire lies in the courage to discard the old and embrace the new.
Selim III believed that the empire's survival depended not on clinging to tradition, but on the ability to reform boldly in response to the changing times.
The strongest ramparts are not made of stone, but of well-trained and loyal soldiers.
He realized the empire could no longer be defended by the old, corrupt Janissaries, and emphasized the importance of a modern, effective military force.
The path of reform is always met with the fiercest resistance.
Through his own experience, he deeply understood that powerful opposition from vested interests was an inevitable fate when pursuing meaningful change.
Ascending the throne amidst chaos, I had to fight both internal and external enemies.
By reforming the army and centralizing power, I reclaimed lost territories and ushered in a golden age for the empire.
